coredata.dc:title: Biomass derived carbon quantum dots embedded PEDOT/CFP electrode for the electrochemical detection of phloroglucinol
coredata.dc:creator: Keerthana, P.
George, Ashlay
Benny, Libina
Varghese, Anitha
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanocomposites have garnered a lot of attention among various nanomaterials due to their distinct characteristics, such as large surface area, biocompatibility, and concise synthetic routes. They are also a viable contender for electrochemical applications, notably sensing, due to their intriguing electrochemical features, which include large electroactive surface area, outstanding electrical conductivity, electrocatalytic activity, and high porosity and adsorption capability. Herein, an electrochemical sensor for phloroglucinol (PL) was designed using a CFP electrode modified with biomass-derived carbon quantum dots (S-CQD) doped on conducting organic polymer poly(3,4-ethylene dioxythiophene) (PEDOT) via electrodeposition method. The obtained nanocomposite (S-CQD+PEDOT) on the CFP electrode possesses a high surface area. The higher electrocatalytic activity of S-CQD and significant conductivity of PEDOT- modified electrode enhance the electrocatalytic activity for the phloroglucinol oxidation. The oxidation peak current of PL shows a higher response on the finally modified electrode than the other electrodes. The developed electrochemical sensor for the selective and sensitive detection of PL showed a good linear range of 36 -360 nM and a detection limit of 11 nM. The modified electrodes were characterized using Transmission electron spectroscopy (TEM), Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and X-ray photon spectroscopy (XPS). Finally, the developed method was successfully used to detect Phloroglucinol from industrial effluents with RSD (0.84–1.02%) and (98.5–101.2%) of recovery.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass-derived
Carbon quantum dots
Conducting polymer
Electrochemical sensor
Phloroglucinol
Pollutant

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of S-CQD, PEDOT/CFP, S-CQD+PEDOT/CFP electrodes
Electrodeposition of S-CQD+PEDOT/CFP on CFP electrode
Determination of phloroglucinol using industrial effluents

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The researchers and authors express their gratitude to the Major research project ( MRP DSC-1830 ), Center for Research, CHRIST (Deemed to be University) , Bangalore, Karnataka, India for providing the required financial support for completing this research work. The authors thank the nanotechnology research center, SRM institute Chennai for their help in the XPS analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Optical properties (b) Raman spectra (c) AFM image of S-CQD.
TEM picture (a) High resolution S-CQD and (b) Low resolution S-CQD (c) Particle size distribution histogram of S-CQD (d, e) TEM picture of S-CQD+PEDOT on CFP electrode.
FT-IR spectrum of (a)PEDOT/CFP, (b) S-CQD, and (c) S-CQD+PEDOT/CFP.
XRD Pattern of (a) S-CQD, (b) PEDOT/CFP and (c) S-CQD+PEDOT/CFP electrode.

(a) Cyclic Voltammogram for electrodeposition of S-CQD+PEDOT/CFP in H2SO4. (0.1 M) and SDS (0.01 M). (b) Cyclic voltammograms (CV) of Bare, PEDOT/CFP and S-CQD+PEDOT/CFP in the potential range −0.4 V and 0.8 V vs SCE at 0.05 V s − 1 at 5 mM [Fe (CN)6 3-/4−] and 1 M KCl. (c) Bare CFP electrode Nyquist plots (d) PEDOT/CFP, and S-CQD+PEDOT/CFP in 5 mM [Fe (CN)6 3−/4−] in 1 M KCl.
XPS spectrum of (a) Survey spectrum (b) C 1 s (c) O 1 s (d) N 1 s (e) S 2p regions of S-CQD+PEDOT/CFP electrode.

(a) CV of 50 nM of PL at S-CQD+PEDOT/CFP electrode at different sweep rates from 0.01 to 0.15 mVs−1 in PBS (pH=7) (b) Calibration graph plot against scan rate and oxidation current (c) Calibration graph for the logarithm of scan rate vs logarithm of anodic peak current.
(a) Cyclic voltammogram (CV) recorded using S-CQD+PEDOT/CFP electrode for 150 nM of PL in 10 mM PBS with pH ranging from 3 to 9. (b) pH vs Current calibration graph for oxidation of PL.

(a) CV of PL (50 nM) at bare, PEDOT/CFP and S-CQD+PEDOT/CFP electrodes in (pH 7) PBS between −0.4 V to 1.2 V at 0.05 V/s. (b) DPVs of PL at various concentrations ranging from 36 nM to 350 nM from the range of 0.35 V to 0.75 V potential vs SCE in PBS (pH 7), (c) calibration plot against concentration of PL Vs anodic peak current for determination of PL.
Interference study of S-CQD+PEDOT/CFP in the presence of various organic pollutants.
represents the synthesis of biomass-derived carbon quantum dots (S-CQD) and design of CFP using biomass-derived carbon quantum dots (S-CQD) and EDOT monomer.
Comparison table of previously reported analytical methods for Phloroglucinol.
Determination of phloroglucinol using industrial effluents.

head.ce:title: Biomass derived carbon quantum dots embedded PEDOT/CFP electrode for the electrochemical detection of phloroglucinol

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, CHRIST (Deemed to be University), Bangalore, Karnataka 560029, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, CHRIST (Deemed to be University), Bangalore, Karnataka 560029, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanocomposites have garnered a lot of attention among various nanomaterials due to their distinct characteristics, such as large surface area, biocompatibility, and concise synthetic routes. They are also a viable contender for electrochemical applications, notably sensing, due to their intriguing electrochemical features, which include large electroactive surface area, outstanding electrical conductivity, electrocatalytic activity, and high porosity and adsorption capability. Herein, an electrochemical sensor for phloroglucinol (PL) was designed using a CFP electrode modified with biomass-derived carbon quantum dots (S-CQD) doped on conducting organic polymer poly(3,4-ethylene dioxythiophene) (PEDOT) via electrodeposition method. The obtained nanocomposite (S-CQD+PEDOT) on the CFP electrode possesses a high surface area. The higher electrocatalytic activity of S-CQD and significant conductivity of PEDOT- modified electrode enhance the electrocatalytic activity for the phloroglucinol oxidation. The oxidation peak current of PL shows a higher response on the finally modified electrode than the other electrodes. The developed electrochemical sensor for the selective and sensitive detection of PL showed a good linear range of 36 -360 nM and a detection limit of 11 nM. The modified electrodes were characterized using Transmission electron spectroscopy (TEM), Fourier Transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and X-ray photon spectroscopy (XPS). Finally, the developed method was successfully used to detect Phloroglucinol from industrial effluents with RSD (0.84–1.02%) and (98.5–101.2%) of recovery.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon materials have gained a lot of attention in recent years because it is a versatile element, its allotropes have particular physicochemical characteristics, making them suitable for use in sensing systems [1,2]. Carbon-based materials such as CNTs [3], fullerenes [4], and graphenes [5] have poor water solubility in nature. Continuous attempts are made to produce a multifunctional material, like carbon quantum dots (CQDs) which possesses a variety of characteristics like crystallinity, shape, texture, surface area and high electrochemical conductivity. Biomass wastes (cultivated residues, leaves, fruits, left-over papers, municipal solid waste, etc.) are used to make CQDs owing to their rich content of carbon (45–55 wt%) [6]. However, processing them from raw biomass is time-consuming. Hydrothermal carbonization (HC) [7–10] and pyrolysis (PY) [11–13] procedures are commonly used for the synthesis of CQD because they are low-cost, active, and appropriate for bulk preparation without the need of refractory solvents. Many functional groups such as hydroxyl (-OH), amino (-NH2), carbonyl (C = O), epoxide, and carboxylic acid (-COOH) moieties at their surface of CQDs (i.e., functionalized CQDs) and reaction sites referred as basal planes, allowing excellent water solubility and stability for subsequent functionalization with various functional groups of molecules.

Conducting polymers provides crucial benefits such as enhanced electrical conductivity, mechanical strength, and good adsorption properties on the surface electrode. Due to their three-dimensional development on the substrate, conducting polymers electrodeposited on substrate electrodes cause porosity and surface roughness, increasing the surface area of the conducting polymer substrate [14,15]. For different electrochemical processes, this promotes electrode-electrolyte interfacial characteristics. These conducting polymers transmit electrons between electrodes and electrolyte solution along polymer chains in electrochemical processes. Biomass-derived carbon quantum dots (S-CQDs) are doped on the conducting polymer surfaces to avoid nanoparticle aggregation. As a result, the surface area enhances and improves the electrooxidation or electro-reduction efficiency [16]. Better-conducting polymers with high conductivity, such as polyacetylene (PA), poly-p-phenylene (PPh), polyaniline (PANI), and poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) (PEDOT) are common supportive electrocatalysts [17,18]. PEDOT is a good conducting host matrix for electrocatalytic purposes due to its high stability, reduced redox potential values, greater electrical properties, and simplicity of production. For the determination and quantification of pollutants, a variety of sensing approaches with various modifications have been used, which is in line with the latest advancements in innovative nanomaterials [19–21].

Organic pollutants (OPs) are hazardous compounds that harm human health and the environment worldwide and show up in a list of hazardous substances of the US Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) [22–25]. Among the various toxic organic pollutants, phenolic and polyphenolic compounds have a place with natural mixtures and are generally conveyed in the environment, including wastewater and normal ecological waters and it is a direct result of their tendency to persist in the environment for a significant time and possess harmful impacts [26]. Releasing these mixtures without treatment might prompt genuine well-being dangers to people, creatures, and amphibian frameworks [27]. The phenolic compound benzene-l,3,5-triol popularly known as phloroglucinol (PL) is found in plants and perform a minor role in various physiological activities [28]. Vomiting, reduced body temperature, faintness, cyanosis, ataxia, unconsciousness, and choking are some symptoms of overuse of PL. Long-term phloroglucinol exposure can cause skin hypersensitivity, dermatitis, anemia, and jaundice, among other things [29].

As a result, developing a quick, easy, and sensitive method for phloroglucinol detection in the environment is of major significance and interest. Although various traditional analytical techniques of detection are accessible, such as spectrophotometry [30], liquid chromatography (LC) [31], and capillary electrophoresis (CE) [32], their primary drawbacks of high costs and low selectivity have placed limitations on their usage. Due to its drawbacks, highly sensitive, very reactive, and economically viable electrochemical sensors with very low detection limits need to be introduced, leading to more advantages usage in pharmaceutical, ecological, biological, and food analyses [33].

In this research work, we have designed a novel electrochemical sensor for monitoring phloroglucinol. This sensor was developed by co-electrodeposition of S-CQD doped with conducting polymer PEDOT (S-CQD+PEDOT). The proposed S-CQD+PEDOT/CFP achieved good electrochemical activity and be employed as an effective electrochemical sensor for a highly sensitive and selective technique of monitoring phloroglucinol in industrial effluents.

Leaves of Solanum Nigrum were collected from a local garden (Tamil Nadu, India). Phloroglucinol and 3,4-Ethylenedioxythiophene (EDOT) monomer were bought from SD Fine-chem Pvt Ltd., India. K3[Fe (CN)6], K2HPO4, K4[Fe (CN)6], and K2HPO4, Conc. Sulphuric acid was purchased from Sigma Aldrich-Merck. All the chemicals and reagents were in pure analytical grade and used with no further purification. All the aqueous solutions were prepared using double distilled (DD)water. Toray carbon fiber paper was built as the working electrode. CFP areas with thicknesses of 0.02 cm, 2.0 cm in length, and 0.7 cm in width were used. One edge of the electrode was introduced into the beaker containing electrolyte solution with an area of 0.7 cm2. A copper wire was connected to the CFP for electrical interaction, and the uncovered region was protected with PTFE tape.

Fourier-Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectrum was used to obtain the surface properties of both the quantum dot and the modified electrode and it was acquired using Thermo Nicolet, Avatar. TEM pictures were recorded by JEOL (JEM-F200) electron magnifying microscope at the voltage of 200 kV. Rigaku Smart Lab X-ray diffractometer utilizing Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Ǻ) to record XRD peaks. Lab RAM HR FT-Raman module was applied to measure peak range at 532 nm LASER. XPS range was recorded using ESCA-14 (Omicron Nanotechnology) X-beam photoelectron spectrometer. Shimadzu UV–Vis spectrophotometer instrument (UV–Vis 1800) was utilized to measure absorption wavelength. Fluorescence (PL) measurements (excitation, emission) were utilized by the Shimadzu spectrofluorometer instrument (RF5301PC). The surface roughness values of bare and the finally improved CFP electrode surfaces were identified by using optical profilometer (KLA-Tencor, US-CA) ZETA-20, Z10002–1–1.

Electrochemical measurements were obtained from Cyclic Voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) technique, and it was recorded by Electrochemical analyzer model CHI609 (CH Instruments, Inc. USA). Electrochemical works were carried out using a conventional three-electrode system. Carbon fiber paper (CFP), modified electrode CFP substrate acts as working electrode, Pt as the counter electrode and Ag/ AgCl-saturated calomel electrode as reference electrode respectively.

The S-CQD was freshly prepared from Solanum nigrum leaves through a green-single step process. The leaves were collected and rinsed continuously using double distilled water to remove the impurities present on the surface of the leaves. Then the leaves were dried in sunlight for 2 days and were pulverized into a powder. Next, 10 g of the fine powder was mixed with 50 ml of water and extracted using the Soxhlet apparatus; the extracted solution was filtered off and poured into a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave, which was kept inside a muffle furnace for 6 hrs at 150 °C. After completion of the reaction, the autoclave was allowed to cool down to room temperature naturally. A concentrated brown solution was centrifuged at 15,000 rpm for 25 mins to remove the unreacted large particles. Then the obtained solution was filtered with 0.22 μM membrane syringe filter. The obtained solution was transferred to a dialysis membrane. Dialysis was carried out for 48 hrs to remove any small unreacted molecules or products due to partial carbonization. The solution obtained was stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator for further studies as represented in Scheme 1 .

By using the Cyclic voltammetric (CV) technique, co-electrodeposition of S-CQD and PEDOT on CFP was carried out. The monomer (EDOT) and S-CQD were oxidized electrochemically using H2SO4 as the electrolyte. Electropolymerization of the monomer EDOT results in the formation of a conducting polymer PEDOT anodic layer. SDS was added into the monomer which acts as a surfactant to help the CFP substrate and conducting polymer (PEDOT) adhere better [34]. The electrolyte solution containing EDOT (0.01 M), S-CQD (16 μg mL–1), H2SO4 (20 ml, 0.1 M) and SDS (0.01 M) into which the bare CFP electrode was introduced and deposited for successive 20 cycles between −1.0 to 2.0 V at a scan rate of 0.05 V/s. After deposition of S-CQD+PEDOT, the as-modified electrode was washed with DD water and dried overnight in a desiccator as represented in Scheme 1.

Electrochemical detection of phloroglucinol (PL) was performed under cyclic voltammetry technique using the finally modified electrode (SCQD+PEDOT/CFP) in the range from +0.4 V to +1.0 V potential at a sweep rate of 0.05 V/s. Various concentrations of PL solution (36 nM - 360 nM) were added to the beaker containing PBS solution (pH=7.0) as an electrolyte. The bare/CFP electrode, displays an irreversible oxidative peak for PL at +0.545 V. The voltammogram for the finally modified electrode is also similar except for a significant increase in the peak current of the anodic peak at +0.545 V and the other peaks corresponds to the surface of the modified electrode. This is owing to the electrode's excellent adsorption of phloroglucinol, as well as the increased surface area as shown in Fig. 9a. The quantitative determination of PL was performed utilizing the DPV approach with a range of +0.2 V to +0.9 V, 0.06 s pulse width, 0.05 V pulse amplitude, and 0.5 s pulse period. For the quantification of PL, the calibration graph was created by displaying the analyte's oxidation peak current at +0.545 V Vs SCE.

To establish the applicability of the modified electrode (SCQD/PEDOT/CFP), it is used for the detection of PL in the different industrial water samples like firework industrial effluent water samples and pharmaceutical industrial effluent water samples under fine-tuned parameters. The collected water samples were filtered with Whatman filter paper. Then the samples were kept at 4 °C for future purpose and allowed to stand for 2 days. An aliquot of the solution was analyzed for the detection of phloroglucinol. PL spiked water samples were also analyzed to evaluate the recovery capacity of the S-CQD+PEDOT/CFP sensor.

The optical properties of S-CQD were identified using UV–Visible spectroscopy as given in Fig. 1 a. The absorption spectra of S-CQD show two peaks such as 273 nm and 370 nm corresponding to π→π* transition (C = C) and n→π* transition (C = O) present in the surface of the carbon quantum dots. Excitation and emission spectra of S-CQD show at 420.23 nm and 476 nm respectively. The inset picture displays green emission under UV light for S-CQD. The Raman spectra of S-CQD show two peaks at 1385 cm−1 and 1518cm−1 due to the D band and G band respectively. The D band corresponds to a distorted carbon atom and the G band represents the graphitic peak of CC atoms in a 2D lattice. The measured ID/IG ratio is 1.72, which confirms the synthesized S-CQD is highly amorphous in nature as shown in Fig. 1b. The Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) analysis was used to identify a height distribution of S-CQD (Fig. 1c). The sizes of S-CQDs appeared in the range of nanometre range and the aggregation property of S-CQD was also identified using an AFM study.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of S-CQD, PEDOT/CFP, S-CQD+PEDOT/CFP electrodes
ce:section.ce:para: TEM analysis was used to do additional research on crystal structure and the variations in microstructures between the materials. The spherical shape of the obtained quantum dots is seen in the TEM images of the as-prepared S-CQDs shown in Fig. 2a. The concentric circles in the SAED pattern (inset) confirms the amorphous nature of the hence-prepared S-CQD. Fig. 2b shows the low-resolution image of S-CQD. The average size of the prepared CQD shows 6.2 nm and it is distributed in the range of 4–8 nm as shown in Fig. 2c. Fig. S1a shows the high-resolution TEM picture of finally modified electrode.Fig. 2d and e shows the finally modified electrode, S-CQD+PEDOT nanocomposite which explains about the interactions between the conducting polymer chains and S-CQD. The orientation of lattice fringes in different direction indicates the polycrystalline nature of the synthesized composite. In the Fig. 2d image the light-coloured region indicates the conducting polymer and dark spherical shape confirms the presence of S-CQD in the synthesized material. Fig. 2e shows d-interplanar spacing and it was found to be ∼0.320 and ∼0.290 nm. The inset SAED image also supports the polycrystalline nature of the synthesized nanocomposite. Usually, electrodeposition of quantum dots tends to be agglomerate on the electrode substrate. In this work, agglomeration property was moderate due to uniform distribution of quantum dots and conducting polymer on CFP electrode.

FT-IR spectroscopy was used to identify the surface functional groups present in S-CQD, PEDOT, and S-CQD+PEDOT and the results were in best agreement with the literature. A comparison of S-CQD, PEDOT, and S-CQD+PEDOT, FT-IR spectrum is shown in Fig. 3 . The major PEDOT peaks were observed in the FTIR spectra, as stated in the literature. The FTIR band at 1355 cm−1 is attributed to stretching vibration of Cα–Cβ, and 1293 cm−1 owing to the thiophene ring of CC or C = C stretching vibration. The C–S bond (thiophene ring) stretching vibrations appeared at 1171 cm−1, 1080 cm−1, 700, and 965 cm−1. A peak at 1020 cm−1 was observed for the Ethylenedioxy group (CO-C) stretching vibration. The ring stretching of the thiophene peak was observed at 1526 cm−1 [34].

For S-CQD, a peak at 3421 cm−1 corresponds to NH and OH broad absorption band, while CN was the other indications for amino groups present at 1157cm−1. The stretching vibration bands at 2920 cm−1 corresponds to CH3 and 2853 cm−1 corresponds to CH2 group. The small bands at 1454 cm−1 (asymmetric) and 1390 cm−1 (symmetric) corresponds to CH bending vibration. The band at 1674 cm−1, attributes to carbonyl (C = O) stretching vibration, whereas the CO and COO− stretching vibration bands present at 1043 cm−1 and 1324 cm−1 respectively. The less stretching vibration band at 1570 cm−1 is revealing C = C combined with the carbonyl peak (C = O) [35].

The S-CQD+PEDOT observed absorption band at 3327.25 cm−1 attributes to the NH and OH bonding stretching vibration between S-CQD and PEDOT, whereas 1096 cm−1 band due to CN vibration. The 2919 and 2857 cm−1 bands are responsible for CH3 and CH2 stretching vibration. The sharp band at 1709 cm−1, attributes to C = O carbonyl stretching vibration, while the bands at 1020 cm−1 correspond to CO band stretching vibration. C = C stretching vibration merged with the carbonyl peak is indicated by the less strong band at 1650 cm−1.Cα–Cβ stretching vibration is responsible for the FTIR band at 1395 cm−1, while CC or C = C stretching of the thiophene ring is accountable for the band at 1293 cm−1. C–S bond stretching vibrations in the thiophene ring are attributed for the peaks at 1192 cm−1 and 1056 cm−1, as well as the peaks between 701 cm−1 and 920 cm−1. The ring stretching peak was observed at 1518cm−1. Thus, the obtained results confirmed that S-CQD was deposited on the PEDOT/CFP electrode.

The XRD pattern of S-CQDs, PEDOT/CFP, and S-CQD+PEDOT/CFP is represented in Fig. 4 . For S-CQD, the XRD peak indicates a single broad peak at about 2θ = 20.1, corresponds to the plane (002) with interlaying space distance (0.39 nm). In-plane diffraction of graphene-like structured S-CQDs was also responsible for the peak centered at 42.64° due to the (101) plane [36]. Furthermore, the decreased intensity and enlarged full-width half maxima shows the amorphous character of the synthesized Carbon dots . Because of the PEDOT's inter-chain planar ring-stacking distance, the XRD pattern (PEDOT/CFP) has unique peaks at 26.4 and 54.5. This pattern is similar to the CFP electrode's XRD pattern, and it was found in all modified electrodes as well. Then the finally modified electrode S-CQD+PEDOT/CFP shows a small hump at 22.4, and 43.6 corresponding to the polycrystalline nature of S-CQD which was deposited on the PEDOT/CFP electrode. The other peak present at 26.1, 54.1 due to the ring-stacking distance of PEDOT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrodeposition of S-CQD+PEDOT/CFP on CFP electrode
ce:section.ce:para: Electrochemical methods were used to monitor the thickness and uniformity of S-CQD and conducting polymer on the electrode surface during deposition. The anodic EDOT oxidation in the first cycle begins at around 0.85 V and produces the poly (3,4-ethylene dioxythiophene) in the first layer. Two oxidation peak currents were found, one at 0.85 V from PEDOT formation due to EDOT oxidation and the other at 1.38 V owing to the further oxidation of PEDOT. Some of the studies described the electrochemical process of co-deposition of S-CQD+EDOT. EDOT monomer and S-CQD oxidize electrochemically in the presence of an acidic supporting electrolyte (H2SO4) to form a layer of S-CQD + PEDOT on the anodic CFP surface. At 0.79 V, the current begins to grow as EDOT oxidizes to PEDOT formation. Current peaks were noticed on the cyclic voltammogram (Fig. 5a) in the range −1.0 and 2.0 V potential vs SCE. While the other redox peaks 0.2486 V and 0.0240 V corresponds to S-CQD. Furthermore, the redox peaks for S-CQD, still maintained at a higher current value, indicating high capability for sensing. This enhances the electrochemical behavior of the S-CQD+PEDOT nanocomposite.

It is one of the quantifiable analysis method for determining the oxidation state of the element, binding energy and elemental composition of the synthesized material. It was used to determine the percentage of various core level peaks of the element, and the deconvoluted peaks as per the state of oxidation. The binding energy of hetero atoms connected to the carbon, including oxygen, sulfur, nitrogen, and halogens, changes.

The S-CQDs deposition on PEDOT/CFP confirmation was identified using XPS analysis as shown in Fig. 6 . The survey spectra of S-CQDs+ PEDOT/CFP revealed four different distinctive peaks of C1s, N1s, O1s, and S 2p with binding energies of 285.2, 400, 531.6 and 165.73 eV respectively as represented in Fig. 6a. Fig. 6b-e results in the major core-level spectrum of S-CQD+ PEDOT/CFP in the C1s, N1s, O1s, and S 2p regions. The percentages of the components C, O, N and S were 74.3%, 18.32%, 1.8%, and 6.2% respectively. The C1s core level has a high resolution of the XPS spectrum shown in Fig. 6b with a high intense peak ascribed to the CC/C = C bond at 284.8 eV. The Peak at 286.93 eV appears due to the carbonyl group which confirms the CO bond in the finally modified electrode. The peak which shows less intense corresponds to the CN bond with binding energy 285.9 eV.

The peak was less intense found to be at 287.0 eV and 288.2 eV is indicative of C-S and carbons of PEDOT. The other proof for the existence of carbonyl group (C = O) in the finally modified electrode (S-CQD+PEDOT/CFP) featured a intense less peak at 289.9 eV. The high-resolution peak of O1s in Fig. 6c shows seven deconvoluted peaks with binding energies as 529.93, 531.4, 531.8, 532.40, 532.80, 533.59, and 533.98 eV. Owing to the presence of carbonyl group on the synthesized biomass-derived S-CQDs surface, the intense peak was observed at 531.4 eV. The COO−bond is present at 533.98 eV due to the carbonyl moieties present on S-CQD's surface. The peak at 533.59 corresponds to hydroxyl (OH) or carbonyl group (CO-C/CO-H) in the modified electrode mainly from the deposition of S-CQD on the PEDOT/CFP electrode. At 529.93 eV, the singly-bound O of PEDOT was detected.

Peaks associated with amorphous O in Pi are observed at 531.8 eV and 533.3 eV. The weakly bonded O on the surface produced a peak at 532.8 eV. The N1s core-level spectra (Fig. 6d) displays a major two distinct peaks, corresponding to NH and CN. The CN bond is represented by the strong, intense peak at 400.49 eV, while the NH bond is represented by another peak at 401.75 eV. The single sulfur (S) bonding in conducting polymer (PEDOT) is confirmed by the XPS core-level high-resolution spectra of S 2p (Fig. 6e). This is supported by the occurrence of two doublets at 166.5 eV and 165.73 eV respectively. The observed asymmetric tail at high binding energy values due to the process of doping, in which the thiophene delocalized electrons expand the binding energy (eV) of the S atom. Hence the above results confirmed the deposition of S-CQD and PEDOT on the CFP electrode [35,36].

To examine the surface roughness, morphology and modified texture on CFP electrode, optical profilometry technique was used. The OP micrographs (338×265 μM region) for bare CFP [37], PEDOT/CFP and S-CQD+PEDOT/CFP were analysed. The effects of deposition were studied in two-dimensional (2D) and three-dimensional (3D) models. Furthermore, the various structural roughness parameters of modified electrodes at the microscale level is listed in Table S1(supporting information). This technique is used for the analysis of the topographical texture of the surface and the modified electrodes do not contact with the instrument. Fig. S2 represents the 2D and 3D forms of the PEDOT/CFP electrode. The film roughness was described in the form of the RMS (root mean square) parameter which was measured to be 10.14 ± 0.4797 μm. Then the deposition of biomass-derived carbon dots and conducting polymer (PEDOT) on the CFP electrode increases the roughness of the electrode found to be 13.19 ± 0.5432 μm as shown in Fig. S2. The surface roughness parameters, Skewness (SSK), kurtosis (SKu), surface roughness square deviation (Sq), valley peak height (Spv), and surface area (Sa) were all listed in Table.S1. The results indicate that the quantum dots play an important role during the deposition on the CFP electrode which clearly in increasing the roughness and hence the surface area.

A comparative study of the bare, PEDOT/CFP, and S-CQD+PEDOT/CFP electrodes was performed using cyclic voltammetry to assess the effectiveness of the surface modification employed. To investigate this, the electrochemical current response was measured using a standard probe, consisting of 5 mM potassium ferro/ferricyanide redox solution with the supporting electrolyte (1 M KCl) [38–40]. The bare, PEDOT/CFP, and S-CQD+PEDOT/CFP modified electrodes were all swept in the range of −0.4 to 0.8 V potential (E) versus SCE at the 50 mV s − 1 scan rate. All the bare and modified electrode surfaces generate definite and separated redox peaks as shown in Fig. 5b.

The conductivity and current value of the modified electrodes subsequently increased due to the porous nature of the CFP electrode. The redox peak produced in Bare CFP with more ΔV of 1.54 V. The catalytic activity of electropolymerized PEDOT on CFP electrode increased with a decrease of ΔV (1.47 V). After deposition of S-CQD+PEDOT on CFP electrode, the current density increased by 4-fold owing to conductivity of rich carbon source of quantum dots. Hence it speeds up the transfer of electrons to the surface of the modified electrode meanwhile ΔV is decreased to 1.1 V. The low ΔV and high current density value prove that S-CQD+PEDOT/CFP electrode has effective catalytic activity. The electroactive surface area of all modified electrodes was calculated using Randle's-Sevcik equation by substituting the slope value from the linear graph with concentration (C), electrode-electrolyte surface area (A) region in cm2, and diffusion coefficient (DO) as given in Eq. (1),
(1)
I p
= 2
. 69 × 1
0
5
A
D
O
1 / 2
n
3 / 2
ν
1 / 2
C

The electroactive surface area of bare, PEDOT/CFP, and S-CQD+PEDOT/CFP modified electrodes were found to be 0.810, 1.72, and 3.64 cm2 respectively. There is a notable increase in the finally modified S-CQD+PEDOT/CFP electrode suggesting high catalytic activity than bare CFP and PEDOT/CFP.

EIS is a useful method for studying the contact properties of modified electrodes with the surface. Impedance spectroscopy is important to determine the dielectric and electric properties of individual contributions to components under investigation. These data were interpreted using two types of plots which are Nyquist and Bode plot. In this work, we used Nyquist plot (Real number vs imaginary numbers) to interpret and estimate the data. This study shows that electron transfer resistance (RCT) equals impedance's semicircle diameter [29]. Furthermore, it was used to learn more about the differences between the three CFP electrodes. The bare CFP, PEDOT/CFP, and S-CQD+PEDOT/CFP Nyquist plots are displayed in (Fig. 5c-d). The data from EIS measurements were analyzed by fitting the signals to Randel's equivalent circuit model. Solution resistance (RS), double layer capacitance (CDL), and the electrode surface resistance to charge transfer (RCT) are the corresponding electrical circuit model. The semi-circular diameter (RCT) values change when modifiers adsorb on the surface of the electrode. About 1198 Ω RCT value was observed for Bare electrode indicating high charge transfer resistance leads to weak sensitivity and low conductivity. A charge transfer resistance (RCT) reduced 78 Ω for PEDOT/CFP electrode possesses high conductivity than the bare electrode. Then the finally modified electrode (S-CQD+PEDOT/CFP) possesses less charge transfer resistance 41 Ω and more conductivity than other electrodes due to carbon-rich quantum dots deposited on the electrode. The above given EIS results suggest that the modified electrode is an effective sensor for detecting PL. The S-CQD+PEDOT/CFP electrode scan rate were investigated. The effect of scan rate depends on the kinetics of electron transfer during the oxidation of PL which was studied [41]. The cyclic voltammetric peaks of 50 nM PL at varying sweep rates ranging from 0.01 to 0.15 Vs−1 are depicted in (Fig. 7 a). In the scan rate 0.01 to 0.15 Vs−1 range, the peak current increased gradually as shown in (Fig. 7b). As demonstrated by the regression equation, an adsorption-controlled process was observed in the oxidation of PL and the increased peak anodic current is primarily owing to the adsorbed surface by the PL analyte.
(2)
i p
= 0
. 00266 v mV / s + 9
. 14838
E
− 5
,
R
2
= 0
. 99371

A linear graph plotted against the anodic logarithm peak currents for PL vs sweep rate logarithm, as illustrated in (Fig. 7c). The slope value obtained from the straight-line equation is extremely close to 1 (the theoretical value). This proves that the adsorption-controlled process at the electrode surface is real. Further support this, the lower diffusion coefficient D (1.6808×10−8) found for estimating PL, which was determined by using Eq. (3).
(3)
i
p
= 0.496 nFC
( α nF / RT )
1 / 2
D
1 / 2
ν
1 / 2
where anodic peak current density is represented by ip, electron coefficient transfer (α), C is the PL concentration and other signs have the usual meaning. Eq. (3) was used to measure the total no. of electrons participated in the PL electro-oxidation mechanism, and was computed as 2.

The solution pH has an important role because it directly relates to the electron transfer between the analyte PL and the modified electrode surface. Cyclic voltammetry was used to optimize the pH of the electrolyte (buffer) solution. The analyte current response of the PL in PBS buffer (20 mM) and PL (150 nM) at the S-CQD+PEDOT/CFP electrode is shown. (Fig. 8 a). While detecting PL, a gradual increase was observed for the oxidation peak current for pH 3 to pH 7, thereafter the current response steadily fell from pH 8–9. This emphasizes the fact that the pH of the supporting electrolyte plays a major role in the oxidation mechanism of the analyte PL. Oxidation peak current decreases at an alkaline pH happened due to deprotonation. Since the oxidation process slows or stops at pH 7, which also leads to the decline in the oxidation peak current, pH 7 is an appropriate electrolyte for the detection and quantification of PL.

The fact that the peak potential changed negatively as the pH increased from 3 to 9 shows that no. of protons contributed in the electrochemical reaction (Fig. 8b). The pH and peak potential relationship was explained in the below given equation:
E p
( V )
= − 0
. 01495 pH + 0
. 0005812 ,
(
R
2
= 0
. 9925
)

According to the Nernst equation, the relationship between the peak potential and pH of an analyte can be determined using Eq. (4)
(4)
E p
= 0
. 0592 m / npH + b

The obtained slope value was very near to the theoretical Nernst value of 0.059 V / pH, indicating that the electrochemical reaction involves a same number of protons and electrons. As a result, PBS with a pH of 7 was used for the current study to achieve high sensitivity.

The range linearity (LR), limit of detection and quantification (LOD), (LOQ) of the PL were identified using the DPV technique. Using this approach, the linear calibration graph for detecting PL was measured with the modified electrode (S-CQD+PEDOT/CFP). For detecting PL at low concentration, all the DPV working limits were optimized to increase the sensitivity. As a result, the parameters were fine-tuned to 0.05 V pulse magnitude, 0.05 s pulse breadth, and 0.05 s pulse period. Cathodic peak current at 0.545 V rises gradually when PL concentration increases from 36 nM to 350 nM, as seen in Fig. 9 b. Peak currents are linearly related to PL concentration, as shown in Fig. 9c calibration graph and the linear regression equation is provided by
(5)
i pa
(
μ A
)
= 5
. 40
× 1
0
− 7
[ PL ]
+ 2
. 233
× 1
0
− 8
;
(
R
2
= 0
. 995
)

The LOQ and LOD values are calculated using the formulas (10 σ /S) and (3.3 σ /S), where S is the calibration graph slope and s is the anodic peak current standard deviation at any given concentration of the analyte. For detecting PL, LOD and LOQ were found to be 11 nM and 36 nM, respectively. Table 1 explains a few detection methodologies that achieved a favorable linear range or detection limit. The proposed S-CQD+PEDOT/CFP modified sensor outperforms compared to previously reported procedures. S-CQD+PEDOT/CFP is a highly sensitive and simple approach that does not require any pre-treatment. Comparison table of previously reported analytical methods for PL as listed in Table 1.

To examine the selectivity of the S-CQD+PEDOT/CFP electrode toward the detection of phloroglucinol (PL); DPV measurements were used to identify the presence of various interfering ions. By adding a sufficient concentration of organic pollutants to a mixture solution containing 150 nM of PL, the probable interferences of several organic pollutants were studied. Organic pollutants such as Resorcinol (RL), Catechol (CL), Metol (ML), Hydroquinone (HQ), Picric acid (PA), 4-nitrophenol, and Bisphenol-A have been used for the interference study. The findings revealed that a 150-fold increase in organic pollutants does not influence peak currents for PL oxidation. This indicates that there were no interference ions for these common species occurred as shown in Fig. 10 .

To determine the repeatability and reproducibility of the sensor, 5 modified electrodes made separately under identical experimental circumstances were monitored and assessed for the oxidation of 150 nM of PL. A relative standard deviation of 2.1 percent was found in the results, indicating excellent repeatability for modified electrodes. In order to identify the accuracy of the developed sensor was investigated using 150 nM of PL. The developed sensor displays the same result after repeated usage more than 10 times and storage stability was measured using the same electrodes after being exposed to the air for 15 days, it managed to hold the 94.5% of the original response which is due to the binding nature of enriched quantum dots and conducting polymer (PEDOT) appeared on electrode's surface. Thereby, these results confirm that the modified electrodes have good storage stability, repeatability, and reproducibility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of phloroglucinol using industrial effluents
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesized S-CQD+PEDOT electrode was used for the determination of PL in the industrial effluent (i.e., firework and pharmaceutical industries) water samples. Water samples from nearby factories that make fireworks and pharmaceuticals were collected and filtered several times using a Whatman-filter paper. Various concentrations of PL were used to spike the water samples, and the resulting solution was then analysed for PL using the DPV method. The recovery values obtained from the DPV technique were compared with the standard method and the range was found to be 98.5–101.2%. Table 2 explains the PL in industrial effluents.

In conclusion, an electrochemical sensor based on the co-electrodeposition of S-CQDs with outstanding electrocatalytic activity and a conducting polymer PEDOT was effectively built. Biomass-derived S-CQDs were distributed homogeneously across the entire conducting polymer nanocomposite as identified from the systematic investigations. The nanocomposite possesses a large specific surface area, low electrochemical impedance, good electrocatalytic activity towards phloroglucinol oxidation with a wide linear range and a low limit of detection. Based on a new nanocomposite of PEDOT doped with S-CQDs, the current work has paved the way for an effective and dependable strategy to achieve high-performance electrochemical sensors. Furthermore, the functional groups carried by S-CQDs, such as carboxyl groups, can be used to construct biosensors. Further the quantitative measurement of PL, the developed sensor shows excellent storage stability, repeatability, and reproducibility. This is an effective material for detecting PL in real sample analysis.

P. Keerthana: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Ashlay George: Conceptualization, Methodology. Libina Benny: Writing – review & editing. Anitha Varghese: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors have no competing interests with the work presented in this manuscript.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The researchers and authors express their gratitude to the Major research project (MRP DSC-1830), Center for Research, CHRIST (Deemed to be University), Bangalore, Karnataka, India for providing the required financial support for completing this research work. The authors thank the nanotechnology research center, SRM institute Chennai for their help in the XPS analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2023.142184.
